item.title: National Disability Employment Awareness Month Celebrated in Workplaces Across NL
item.dc:creator: Sara Strickland
item.description:
Well b'y, looks like the province is makin' a big to-do by lightin' everything up in purple for National Disability Employment Awareness Month. It's all part of this "Light it Up for NDEAM" campaign. They're givin' a nod to all the hard work folks with disabilities are doin' in the workforce.
That fella, Sean Wiltshire, who's at the helm of Avalon Employment, says they've got a slew of programs to match employers with folks lookin' for jobs. They've even got this mentorship thing where you can try out a job to see if it's a fit, like testin' the waters without getting your feet wet. He calls it "Mentor Abilities," and it's supposed to give folks with disabilities the inside scoop on jobs. Apparently, it helps people make sense of their career choices or figure out if they need more training.
Now, I'm all for helpin' folks find work, don't get me wrong. But sometimes I wonder if the bigwigs lightin' up the buildings are more about flash than real change. Mind you, if it leads to more opportunities for those who need 'em, I'm all for it. So, hats off to Avalon Employment, hope their programs do what they say on the tin!
item.content:encoded:
Well b'y, looks like the province is makin' a big to-do by lightin' everything up in purple for National Disability Employment Awareness Month. It's all part of this "Light it Up for NDEAM" campaign. They're givin' a nod to all the hard work folks with disabilities are doin' in the workforce.
That fella, Sean Wiltshire, who's at the helm of Avalon Employment, says they've got a slew of programs to match employers with folks lookin' for jobs. They've even got this mentorship thing where you can try out a job to see if it's a fit, like testin' the waters without getting your feet wet. He calls it "Mentor Abilities," and it's supposed to give folks with disabilities the inside scoop on jobs. Apparently, it helps people make sense of their career choices or figure out if they need more training.
Now, I'm all for helpin' folks find work, don't get me wrong. But sometimes I wonder if the bigwigs lightin' up the buildings are more about flash than real change. Mind you, if it leads to more opportunities for those who need 'em, I'm all for it. So, hats off to Avalon Employment, hope their programs do what they say on the tin!

item.title: Collision Clinic Gearing Up to Award Car to Deserving Individual
item.dc:creator: Sara Strickland
item.description:
Ah, b'ys, would ya look at that! The Collision Clinic over in St. John's is doin' somethin' mighty kind. They're takin' them busted-up cars – the ones most folks would leave for scrap – and fixin' 'em up real nice for someone who needs a set of wheels but can't quite afford 'em. A real heart-warmin' thing, if I'm honest. Their crew works on their own time to get these vehicles back on the road, so you know they're doin' it because they care, not for any o' the bucks.
Now, here's the kicker: they give the car to whoever can show how not havin' a reliable ride is makin' their life a right pain. So, if you're sick of hitchin' rides off your buddy or your auntie, maybe this is your chance. Go on and get your application in before November 24th. Those fellas know how to put a good show on for the folks who need it most, even if the process ain't exactly a Sunday drive down the Parkway!
item.content:encoded:
Ah, b'ys, would ya look at that! The Collision Clinic over in St. John's is doin' somethin' mighty kind. They're takin' them busted-up cars – the ones most folks would leave for scrap – and fixin' 'em up real nice for someone who needs a set of wheels but can't quite afford 'em. A real heart-warmin' thing, if I'm honest. Their crew works on their own time to get these vehicles back on the road, so you know they're doin' it because they care, not for any o' the bucks.
Now, here's the kicker: they give the car to whoever can show how not havin' a reliable ride is makin' their life a right pain. So, if you're sick of hitchin' rides off your buddy or your auntie, maybe this is your chance. Go on and get your application in before November 24th. Those fellas know how to put a good show on for the folks who need it most, even if the process ain't exactly a Sunday drive down the Parkway!

item.title: Member of St. John’s Downtown Business Community Being Remembered
item.dc:creator: Sara Strickland
item.description: Oh b'y, there goes another one of the good ones from downtown. Irene Murphy's gone and passed on, bless her heart. Irene's Souvenirs & Gift Shop on Water Street was her pride and joy for 29 years, and we all know it wasn't just another tourist trap; it actually had stuff you'd wanna buy, right? She and her hubby George had the ol' Murphy's on St. Thomas' Line before that. George kicked the bucket in 2013, and Irene kept on runnin' the show with her daughter Wanda. Hopefully Wanda keeps the charm alive on Water Street. Betcha folks will be popping in for a yarn and some mementos for years to come. Rest in peace, Irene, you did good.
item.content:encoded: Oh b'y, there goes another one of the good ones from downtown. Irene Murphy's gone and passed on, bless her heart. Irene's Souvenirs & Gift Shop on Water Street was her pride and joy for 29 years, and we all know it wasn't just another tourist trap; it actually had stuff you'd wanna buy, right? She and her hubby George had the ol' Murphy's on St. Thomas' Line before that. George kicked the bucket in 2013, and Irene kept on runnin' the show with her daughter Wanda. Hopefully Wanda keeps the charm alive on Water Street. Betcha folks will be popping in for a yarn and some mementos for years to come. Rest in peace, Irene, you did good.

item.title: NAPE Encountering ‘Complex’ Issues Negotiating Some Contracts
item.dc:creator: Sara Strickland
item.description:
Well, b'y, this one's a head-scratcher. The folks at NAPE are tryin' to hammer out some contracts, but it seems like the crowd holding the purse strings, that'd be the provincial government, can't be arsed to show up at the bargaining table. Instead, they're leaving it to everyone else to sort the mess.
Jerry Earle, who you'd think has seen it all, is just baffled by this. He's scratching his noggin' wonderin' how on Earth they expect to get things sorted when the ones payin' the bills are sittin' out like a bunch of invisible fiddlers. It's like trying to negotiate with your shadow, or worse, trying to talk sense into a puffin!
And I tell ya, figuring out contracts in the home care sector is like trying to untangle a cod net after the storm – messy and all over the shop. One thing's for sure, 'tis no easy task, especially when decision-makers are as scarce as a warm breeze in February.
item.content:encoded:
Well, b'y, this one's a head-scratcher. The folks at NAPE are tryin' to hammer out some contracts, but it seems like the crowd holding the purse strings, that'd be the provincial government, can't be arsed to show up at the bargaining table. Instead, they're leaving it to everyone else to sort the mess.
Jerry Earle, who you'd think has seen it all, is just baffled by this. He's scratching his noggin' wonderin' how on Earth they expect to get things sorted when the ones payin' the bills are sittin' out like a bunch of invisible fiddlers. It's like trying to negotiate with your shadow, or worse, trying to talk sense into a puffin!
And I tell ya, figuring out contracts in the home care sector is like trying to untangle a cod net after the storm – messy and all over the shop. One thing's for sure, 'tis no easy task, especially when decision-makers are as scarce as a warm breeze in February.

item.title: Local Music Community Raising Concerns Over Recent Changes at East Coast Music Association
item.dc:creator: Sara Strickland
item.description:
Oh b'y, looks like the East Coast Music Awards are stirring the pot a bit, eh? They're supposed to be havin' the next big shindig right here in St. John's come spring, but instead of us all getting’ ready to party, folks are up in arms about what the heck's going on with the ECMA's leadership. Apparently, they've been making some big changes without much of a heads-up to the folks it actually matters to—like, y'know, the musicians and industry people right here in Atlantic Canada.
There's a petition going around calling for "transparency and stability." Sounds like things are about as clear as a foggy morning down by the harbor. They're waving goodbye to some folks with a whole whack of experience, and changing up how they handle applications for the awards, and not in a good way, so it seems. Now, everyone's getting all uneasy and confused 'cause the higher-ups have barely muttered a word to explain themselves.
People just want them to stop muckin' around with a strategy clearer than a bottle of screech and actually lay out their plans. Here's hoping they sort themselves out before they come over spring, or else it'll be quite the scene, sure.
item.content:encoded:
Oh b'y, looks like the East Coast Music Awards are stirring the pot a bit, eh? They're supposed to be havin' the next big shindig right here in St. John's come spring, but instead of us all getting’ ready to party, folks are up in arms about what the heck's going on with the ECMA's leadership. Apparently, they've been making some big changes without much of a heads-up to the folks it actually matters to—like, y'know, the musicians and industry people right here in Atlantic Canada.
There's a petition going around calling for "transparency and stability." Sounds like things are about as clear as a foggy morning down by the harbor. They're waving goodbye to some folks with a whole whack of experience, and changing up how they handle applications for the awards, and not in a good way, so it seems. Now, everyone's getting all uneasy and confused 'cause the higher-ups have barely muttered a word to explain themselves.
People just want them to stop muckin' around with a strategy clearer than a bottle of screech and actually lay out their plans. Here's hoping they sort themselves out before they come over spring, or else it'll be quite the scene, sure.

item.title: Building Code Violations Against Celtics Corp. Dropped
item.dc:creator: Sara Strickland
item.description:
Oh me nerves, have ya heard about the D.F. Barnes Arena down in St. John’s? The Avalon Celtics Minor Hockey crowd, who own the place, were knee-deep in hot water with 19 bylaw violations slapped on 'em. They must’ve had more problems than a flipper pie on a Friday night: dodgy emergency lights, wobbly hand rails, and some funky ventilation issues to boot.
Seems like they’ve shown the city they’re a good crew, though, and fixed up all them issues, ‘cause the charges have been tossed out. Fair play to them! The city’s helped them out with a whack of money before, and even more recently, they’ve dipped into their pockets again to clear a $2.3 million tab.
Guess when ya look after the arena, the city’ll look after you, eh? Just hope they’ve got their act together now, or they might be skatin’ on thin ice again soon enough!
item.content:encoded:
Oh me nerves, have ya heard about the D.F. Barnes Arena down in St. John’s? The Avalon Celtics Minor Hockey crowd, who own the place, were knee-deep in hot water with 19 bylaw violations slapped on 'em. They must’ve had more problems than a flipper pie on a Friday night: dodgy emergency lights, wobbly hand rails, and some funky ventilation issues to boot.
Seems like they’ve shown the city they’re a good crew, though, and fixed up all them issues, ‘cause the charges have been tossed out. Fair play to them! The city’s helped them out with a whack of money before, and even more recently, they’ve dipped into their pockets again to clear a $2.3 million tab.
Guess when ya look after the arena, the city’ll look after you, eh? Just hope they’ve got their act together now, or they might be skatin’ on thin ice again soon enough!

item.title: Final Copy of Review into Long-Term Care Facilities Expected in Coming Months
item.dc:creator: Sara Strickland
item.description:
Well now, b'ys, here's the scoop on this one. The government got the first crack at a draft report about the long-term care homes in Newfoundland and Labrador. Back last year, people started hollering for this after the Senior’s Advocate called for a deep dive into these homes where ya put Nan and Pop. There were some real tearjerkers and more than a few things that just weren't right when you talked to the families, kinda like getting a rotten fish at the market.
The job of poking around in these issues was handed off to MNP, some big outfit, last November. Our buddy Tom Osborne, who was health minister at the time—God love him—was saying back in March that the report was just around the corner. But here we are, still waiting, like for a decent day in April to get out fishing. They say the data's all collected, and it's being looked at by these so-called experts.
So, in good ol' government fashion, we're told to expect something in the "coming months." Not in the "coming days" or even "weeks," but "months"—so don't hold your breath. They're making more promises than the North Atlantic's unpredictable weather, if you ask me. Time will tell, but let's hope the recommendations actually do some good and aren't just thrown to the gulls, eh?
item.content:encoded:
Well now, b'ys, here's the scoop on this one. The government got the first crack at a draft report about the long-term care homes in Newfoundland and Labrador. Back last year, people started hollering for this after the Senior’s Advocate called for a deep dive into these homes where ya put Nan and Pop. There were some real tearjerkers and more than a few things that just weren't right when you talked to the families, kinda like getting a rotten fish at the market.
The job of poking around in these issues was handed off to MNP, some big outfit, last November. Our buddy Tom Osborne, who was health minister at the time—God love him—was saying back in March that the report was just around the corner. But here we are, still waiting, like for a decent day in April to get out fishing. They say the data's all collected, and it's being looked at by these so-called experts.
So, in good ol' government fashion, we're told to expect something in the "coming months." Not in the "coming days" or even "weeks," but "months"—so don't hold your breath. They're making more promises than the North Atlantic's unpredictable weather, if you ask me. Time will tell, but let's hope the recommendations actually do some good and aren't just thrown to the gulls, eh?

item.title: New Organization Helping Newcomers Enter Workforce
item.dc:creator: Sara Strickland
item.description:
Well, b’ys, there's a new gig at MUN’s Faculty of Business that's tryin' to help out them newcomers to the province, eh? It’s called the Expertise Hub Co-operative, and it popped up right during Co-operative week. The idea is to help folks who come to Newfoundland and Labrador with fancy-pants degrees—your master's and PhDs and whatnot—but can't find a job to match their smarts.
This Roxana Fazli, she’s the co-founder, says they’ll partner with other groups and whip up programs to bridge the gap, and they'll also line up short-term gigs for newcomers. They're hopin’ to find them work with governments, charities, and businesses that need someone with specific skills without takin’ them on full-time.
Now, it’s about time someone did somethin’ about this, 'cause last I checked, a person’s educashun doesn’t vanish when they cross over the ferry. But then again, who’s to say if this’ll work? Hopefully, we’ll see more smart folks stayin' here instead of moving away to find work. And wouldn’t that be a nice change for once, eh?
item.content:encoded:
Well, b’ys, there's a new gig at MUN’s Faculty of Business that's tryin' to help out them newcomers to the province, eh? It’s called the Expertise Hub Co-operative, and it popped up right during Co-operative week. The idea is to help folks who come to Newfoundland and Labrador with fancy-pants degrees—your master's and PhDs and whatnot—but can't find a job to match their smarts.
This Roxana Fazli, she’s the co-founder, says they’ll partner with other groups and whip up programs to bridge the gap, and they'll also line up short-term gigs for newcomers. They're hopin’ to find them work with governments, charities, and businesses that need someone with specific skills without takin’ them on full-time.
Now, it’s about time someone did somethin’ about this, 'cause last I checked, a person’s educashun doesn’t vanish when they cross over the ferry. But then again, who’s to say if this’ll work? Hopefully, we’ll see more smart folks stayin' here instead of moving away to find work. And wouldn’t that be a nice change for once, eh?

item.title: Newfoundland Power Develops New Eco-Friendly Mineral Oil Replacement
item.dc:creator: Sara Strickland
item.description:
Well b'ys, seems like Newfoundland Power's makin' a bit of a splash, eh? They've cooked up some newfangled biodegradable goo to replace the regular mineral oil in them transformers. Now, ya probably already know, them oils ain't too friendly when they decide to leak out all over God's green earth—costs a pretty penny to clean up the mess too.
So, now they've got these fancy synthetic esters, and they even managed to nab a cool 101 grand from the government to try 'em out. They say it's a "significant step"—but who knows, maybe they're just lookin' to green-wash their image, right? Personally, I guess it's good they're tryin' to keep the ocean from turnin' into an oily soup, but we'll see if this pilot project actually holds water. If it helps keep our fishin' and currant drinkin' safe, I say good on 'em!
item.content:encoded:
Well b'ys, seems like Newfoundland Power's makin' a bit of a splash, eh? They've cooked up some newfangled biodegradable goo to replace the regular mineral oil in them transformers. Now, ya probably already know, them oils ain't too friendly when they decide to leak out all over God's green earth—costs a pretty penny to clean up the mess too.
So, now they've got these fancy synthetic esters, and they even managed to nab a cool 101 grand from the government to try 'em out. They say it's a "significant step"—but who knows, maybe they're just lookin' to green-wash their image, right? Personally, I guess it's good they're tryin' to keep the ocean from turnin' into an oily soup, but we'll see if this pilot project actually holds water. If it helps keep our fishin' and currant drinkin' safe, I say good on 'em!

item.title: Petition to Salvage Quidi Vidi Slipway Presented at City Council
item.dc:creator: Sara Strickland
item.description:
Ah, b'ys, it's just like what's goin' on over in Quidi Vidi. Y'know that ol’ wooden slipway they’ve got there, right? Well, seems the city folks decided it's done fer and reckon it's about time it gets the boot. But the locals ain't having it. They've got over 800 of 'em signin’ a petition wanting to keep ‘er, repair, or replace it, not just cart it off to the dump.
Councillor Ophelia Ravencroft took the petition to the city council, reading how folks round those parts still launch their boats off it and consider it quite the attraction for tourists. A fine piece of the cultural puzzle of that quaint little harbour, don't ya know? She even quoted the petition saying how any bit of their history that's left has been torn away or sold off to developers. What a surprise, eh?
They had some public feedback over the summer, but sounds like everyone's chattin’ in circles about it. The city oughta realize that sometimes a good bit of old wood is worth more than any fancy development they could think of. But odds are, they'll figure a long chat about how to keep access to the water without spending too much. Typical, right? Just keep your fingers crossed they don’t mess it up this time!
item.content:encoded:
Ah, b'ys, it's just like what's goin' on over in Quidi Vidi. Y'know that ol’ wooden slipway they’ve got there, right? Well, seems the city folks decided it's done fer and reckon it's about time it gets the boot. But the locals ain't having it. They've got over 800 of 'em signin’ a petition wanting to keep ‘er, repair, or replace it, not just cart it off to the dump.
Councillor Ophelia Ravencroft took the petition to the city council, reading how folks round those parts still launch their boats off it and consider it quite the attraction for tourists. A fine piece of the cultural puzzle of that quaint little harbour, don't ya know? She even quoted the petition saying how any bit of their history that's left has been torn away or sold off to developers. What a surprise, eh?
They had some public feedback over the summer, but sounds like everyone's chattin’ in circles about it. The city oughta realize that sometimes a good bit of old wood is worth more than any fancy development they could think of. But odds are, they'll figure a long chat about how to keep access to the water without spending too much. Typical, right? Just keep your fingers crossed they don’t mess it up this time!
